elements.sent-2411-001: This article summarizes our recent efforts to understand spontaneous quantum vacuum forces and torques, which require that a stationary object be out of thermal equilibrium with the blackbody background radiation.
elements.sent-2411-002: We proceed by a systematic expansion in powers of the electric susceptibility.
elements.sent-2411-003: In first order, no spontaneous force can arise, although a torque can appear, but only if the body is composed of nonreciprocal material.
elements.sent-2411-004: In second order, both forces and torques can appear, with ordinary materials, but only if the body is inhomogeneous.
elements.sent-2411-005: In higher orders, this last requirement may be removed.
elements.sent-2411-006: We give a number of examples of bodies displaying second-order spontaneous forces and torques, some of which might be amenable to observation.
elements.sent-2411-007: Growing out of our explorations of quantum (Casimir) friction,\cite{eqfi,eqfii,qfpc we are studying the forces and torques arising on bodies in vacuum out of thermal equilibrium.}
elements.sent-2411-008: A torque can arise in first order in susceptibility for a body that is nonreciprocal.
elements.sent-2411-009: This is described in more detail in Refs.~\citen{nr,kennedy .}
elements.sent-2411-010: For a reciprocal body, spontaneous torques and forces require not only nonequilibrium but inhomogeneity of the body.
elements.sent-2411-011: Such effects emerge in second order.
elements.sent-2411-012: More detail on spontaneous forces and torques can be found in Refs.~\citen{selfprop, sptorque .}
elements.sent-2411-013: See these papers for fuller references to the literature.
elements.section-2411-002: Quantum Vacuum Torque in Nonreciprocal Media
elements.sent-2411-015: This phenomenon was first discussed in Ref.~\citen{Strekha , and then subsequently in our paper, Ref.~\citen{nr .}}
elements.sent-2411-018: The first term here is called the {\it internal\/ torque and the second the {\it external\/ torque, because the latter is reflective of the force on the body.}}
elements.sent-2411-019: We expand this out to first order in generalized susceptibilities, using \begin{equation}\mathbf{E}^{(1)}(\mathbf{r};\omega)=\int (d\mathbf{r'})\bm{\Gamma} (\mathbf{r-r'};\omega)\cdot \mathbf{P}(\mathbf{r}';\omega),\quad \mathbf{P}^{(1)}(\mathbf{r};\omega)=\bm{\chi}(\mathbf{r};\omega) \cdot\mathbf{E}(\mathbf{r};\omega),\end{equation} in terms of the (local) electric susceptibility $\bm{\chi}$ and the (vacuum) electromagnetic Green's dyadic $\bm{\Gamma}$.
elements.sent-2411-021: Here, $\bm{\Gamma}$ is taken to be the usual vacuum retarded Green's dyadic, which at coincident points is (rotationally averaged) \begin{equation}\bm{\Gamma}(\mathbf{r-r'};\omega) \to\bm{1}\left(\frac{\omega^2}{6\pi R}+i\frac{\omega^3}{6\pi}+O(R)\right), \quad R=|\mathbf{r-r'}|\to0,\end{equation} while $\bm{\chi ^A$ is the anti-Hermitian part of the susceptibility: \begin{equation}\bm{\chi}^A=\frac1{2i}(\bm{\chi}-\bm{\chi}^\dagger), \quad \Im\bm{\chi}^A_{ij}(\omega)= -\frac12\Re[\chi_{ij}(\omega)-\chi_{ji}(\omega)],\end{equation} which displays the antisymmetric part of $\bm{\chi ^{A}$, which is even in $\omega$.}}
elements.sent-2411-022: The real part of $\chi^A$ is symmetric in indices and odd in $\omega$.
elements.sent-2411-023: Only the former contributes to the torque.
elements.sent-2411-024: Using this, we readily calculate the torque on a nonreciprocal body in vacuum, due to PP and EE fluctuations: \begin{equation}\tau_i=-\int_{-\infty}^\infty \frac{d\omega}{2\pi} \frac{\omega^3}{6\pi} \left[\coth\frac{\beta'\omega}2-\coth\frac{\beta\omega}2\right]\epsilon_{ijk} \Re\alpha_{jk}(\omega),\end{equation} where the mean polarizability of the body is given by $\alpha_{jk (\omega)=\int(d\mathbf{r )\chi_{jk (r;\omega)$.}}}
elements.sent-2411-025: This result exactly agrees with that of Strekha et al.\cite{Strekha However, there is no quantum vacuum force in this static situation.}
elements.sent-2411-026: To create a nonreciprocal medium typically requires an external magnetic field, so this is not exactly a pure vacuum phenomenon.
elements.sent-2411-027: If the nonreciprocity is generated by a magnetic field of 1 T, the corresponding torque for a gold nanoball of radius 100 nm is $\sim 10^{-24}$ N\,m for $T'=600$~K, $T=300$ K.
elements.sent-2411-029: Consider a body composed of two parts, each of which is separately made of homogeneous material.
elements.sent-2411-031: The susceptibility of a thin needle of cross section $S$ with parts $A$ and $B$ of length $a$ and $b$ made of different materials is \begin{equation}\chi(\mathbf{r};\omega)=S\left[\delta(x)\delta(y)\theta(z)\theta(a-z) \chi_A^{\vphantom{q}} (\omega)+\delta(x)\delta(y)\theta(-z)\theta(b+z)\chi_B^{\vphantom{q}}(\omega) \right].\end{equation}.
elements.sent-2411-032: For definiteness, chose $\chi^{\vphantom{q _A$ to be a real constant (frequencies small compared to binding energies), while $B$ is taken to be a Drude-type metal \begin{equation}\chi_B^{\vphantom{q}}(\omega)=-\frac{\omega_p^2}{\omega^2+i\omega\nu}. \label{drude}\end{equation} For gold, with the vacuum at $T=300$ K, and the cross-sectional radius of the needle being 1 mm, the force is about ($a_0=1$ cm, $\beta_0=40$ (eV)$^{-1}$) \begin{equation}F=-\frac{S^2\omega_p^2\nu\chi^{\vphantom{q}}_A}{120\pi^3} \frac{\beta_0^2}{a_0^5}\hat{F}= -2 \chi^{\vphantom{q}}_A \times 10^{-20 }\hat{F}\,N,\end{equation} where $\hat{F}$ for gold is shown in Figs.~\ref{fig2 and \ref{fig3 .}}}}
elements.sent-2411-033: Since $\hat F\sim 10^{18}$, this seems very large.
elements.figure-2411-001: \begin{minipage}{.4\textwidth} \includegraphics[width=5cm]{needleFrx.pdf} \caption{Force ($\hat F$) on inhomogeneous needle for $a=b$ when $T=300$ K and $T'=600$ K. Note the saturation for large $a$; only the immediate region near the $A$-$B$ interface contributes. The saturated force ($F$) is about $0.03 \chi^{\vphantom{q}}_A$ N.} \label{fig2} \end{minipage} \begin{minipage}{.4\textwidth} \includegraphics[width=5cm]{INFx.pdf} \caption{Dependence of force ($\hat{F}$) on $T'$ for $T=300$ K. $a=b=1$ cm.} \vskip1.2cm \label{fig3} \end{minipage}
elements.sent-2411-034: However, this is probably overly optimistic.
elements.sent-2411-035: For the validity of our weak-susceptibility model, we need the needle to be much thinner than 1 mm.
elements.sent-2411-036: The appropriate scale would seem to be the skin depth, which is\cite{CE \begin{equation}\delta=(\omega \sigma/2)^{-1/2}=(\omega^2\Im\chi/2)^{-1/2} =\sqrt{\frac{2(\omega^2+\nu^2)}{\omega\omega_p^2\nu}}\sim 50 \,nm,\end{equation} which reduces this force substantially (by a factor of $10^{-17}$).}
elements.sent-2411-037: Consider next a thin spherical shell of radius $a$ and thickness $t$.
elements.sent-2411-038: Suppose the upper and lower hemispheres have different uniform susceptibilities \begin{equation}\chi(\mathbf{r};\omega)=\left\{\begin{array}{cc} 0<\theta<\frac{\pi}2: \chi_A^{\vphantom{q}}(\omega), \frac{\pi}2<\theta<\pi: \chi_B^{\vphantom{q}}(\omega).\end{array}\right.\end{equation}.}
elements.sent-2411-039: In this case the geometric integral $I_{AB}$ is shown in Fig.~\ref{fig4 .}
elements.figure-2411-002: \begin{minipage}{.45\textwidth} \includegraphics[width=5cm]{revFig4r2.pdf} \caption{Numerical integration of $I_{AB}$, apart from the prefactor $\omega^8 a^5 t/(8\pi)$, is shown by the dots. The solid line shows that the asymptotic value of $\phi(v)\sim-v^4$ leads to the power-law behavior $\mathcal{N}/(\omega a)^4$ with $\mathcal{N}\approx-27$.} \label{fig4} \end{minipage} \begin{minipage}{.45\textwidth} \includegraphics[width=5cm]{SphForcerx.pdf} \caption{Dimensionless force $\hat F$ as a function of the temperature of the spherical shell relative to the room-temperature background.} \vskip1cm \label{fig5} \end{minipage}
elements.sent-2411-040: The force on the thin spherical shell is \begin{equation}F=-\frac{\omega_p^2 t^2 a}{16\pi^2}\chi_A^{\vphantom{q}}\nu^3 \mathcal{N} \hat{F}\approx1 \times 10^{-12}\chi^{\vphantom{q}}_A \hat{F}\,N \label{forcesph}\end{equation} for gold, with the thickness of the shell being $t=2/\omega_p$ (minimum value of skin depth), and the radius of the shell being $a=1$ cm.
elements.sent-2411-041: The dimensionless force $\hat F$ is shown in Fig.~\ref{fig5 .. {\it Example 3.}}
elements.sent-2411-042: We now consider a ball, of radius $a$, each half-ball, $A$ and $B$, being composed of a different homogeneous material.
elements.sent-2411-043: Then the geometric integral for such a ball is shown in Fig.~\ref{fig6 .}
elements.figure-2411-003: \begin{minipage}{.45\textwidth} \includegraphics[width=6cm]{revFig6r.pdf} \caption{The geometric integral for a Janus ball, scaled by $8\pi a$, is given by the dots, as a function of $\omega a$. The straight lines show the large $\sim(\omega a)^4$ and small $\sim( \omega a)^8$ behaviors.} \vskip.2cm \label{fig6} \end{minipage} \begin{minipage}{.5\textwidth} \includegraphics[width=6cm]{smallJBx.pdf} \caption{Semilog plot of the magnitude of the force on a Janus ball as a function of the temperature of the ball, relative to that of the room-temperature background. The force is negative if the ball is hotter than the blackbody background.} \label{fig7} \end{minipage}
elements.sent-2411-044: The spontaneous force on a small Janus ball of radius $1\, \mu$m, with $A$ inert and $B$ gold, is \begin{equation}F^{\rm JB}=\frac1{27\pi}\chi^{\vphantom{q}}_A\omega_p^2(\nu a)^7 \hat{F}\approx 4 \times 10^{-18} \chi^{\vphantom{q}}_A\hat{F} \,N.\label{JBpre}\end{equation} The dimensionless force is shown in Fig.~\ref{fig7 for $T=300$ K.}
elements.sent-2411-045: The result may be compared with the nonperturbative numerical results of Ref.~\citen{Reid ; the numbers are similar, but the scaling with $a$ is different.}
elements.sent-2411-046: We consider a thin planar object, which consists of blackbody material on the upper side, $A$, and a Drude metal on the lower, $B$.
elements.sent-2411-047: By a blackbody material we mean one whose surface susceptibility, $\tilde{\chi =\frac{V S\chi$, $V$ and $S$ being the volume and the surface area of the body, is \begin{equation}\tilde{\chi}=\frac{i}4\frac{1}{\omega+i\epsilon},\quad \epsilon\to+0,\end{equation} since this will yield Stefan's law for the radiated power, $P=S\pi^2(T^4-T^{\prime 4 )/60$.}}}
elements.sent-2411-048: If the thicknesses of the two sides are $t_A$ and $t_B$, the force is \begin{equation}F_z=\frac{S t_B(t_A+t_B)\omega_p^2 \nu^4}{24\pi^2}\hat{F},\label{JPF1}\end{equation} where $\hat{F}$ is shown in Fig.~\ref{fig8 .}
elements.sent-2411-049: For $S=1$ cm$^2$, $t_A=t_B=10$ nm, and the metal being gold, the prefactor in the force is $4\times 10^{-13}$ N, corresponding to an acceleration of $\sim 40$~m/s$^2$.
elements.sent-2411-050: Comparable results were found in Ref.~\citen{Manjavacas .}
elements.sent-2411-051: In this case, as in the other examples, the force is always towards the metal side of the object (in our convention, negative).
elements.subsection-2411-001: Friction and thermalization lead to terminal velocity
elements.sent-2411-052: Once the body starts to move due to the nonequilibrium quantum force, it will experience quantum friction, nonrelativistically given by the Einstein-Hopf formula, \begin{equation}F_f=-\frac{v\beta}{12\pi^2}\int_0^\infty d\omega\,\omega^5 \Im\alpha(\omega)\frac1{\sinh^2\beta\omega/2},\end{equation} and, therefore, it will reach a terminal velocity: \begin{equation}v(t)=v^{\vphantom{q}}_T\left(1-e^{-t/t_0}\right).\end{equation}.
elements.sent-2411-053: For example, a needle having a 1 cm half-length and 10 nm radius, would have a terminal velocity of order 5 m/s.
elements.sent-2411-054: But the time scale, $t_0 \sim 15$ yrs, is very long.
elements.sent-2411-056: Unless there is some mechanism supplied to maintain the temperature difference, the body will eventually cool to the temperature of the environment.
elements.sent-2411-057: From Newton's law, the terminal velocity is \begin{equation}v^{\vphantom{q}}_T=\frac1m \int_0^\infty dt\, F(T'(t), T).\label{tervelcool}\end{equation} Here, the cooling rate is given by \begin{equation}\frac{dQ}{dt}=C_V(T')\frac{dT'}{dt}=P(T',T),\end{equation} where $C_V(T')$ is the specific heat of the body at temperature $T'$.
elements.sent-2411-058: For a slowly moving body the power radiated is\cite{eqfii \begin{equation}P(T',T)= \frac1{3\pi^2}\int_0^\infty d\omega\,\omega^4\Im \tr\mbox{\boldmath{$\alpha$}}(\omega)\left[ \frac1{e^{\beta\omega}-1}-\frac1{e^{\beta'\omega}-1}\right].\end{equation}.}
elements.sent-2411-059: In terms of $u=T'/T$, the time taken for the body to cool from temperature $T_0'$ to temperature $T_1'$, $T_0'>T_1'>T$, is (for temperatures well above the Debye temperature) \begin{equation}t_1=\int_{T_0'}^{T'_1} dT'\frac{C_V(T')}{P(T',T)}, \quad or\quad \frac{t_1}{t_c}=\int_{u_0}^{u_1} du \frac1{p(u;T)},\end{equation} where the dimensionless power radiated is modeled by\cite{nr \begin{equation}p(u;T)=\int_0^\infty dx\frac{x^3}{x^2+1}\left(\frac1{e^{\beta\nu x}-1}- \frac1{e^{\beta\nu x/u}-1}\right).\label{p}\end{equation} Therefore, the terminal velocity is \begin{equation}v^{\vphantom{q}}_T=\frac{t_c}m \int_{u_0}^1 du \frac{ F(u;T)}{p(u;T)}.\end{equation}.}
elements.sent-2411-060: The time scale for a weak susceptibility model of the metal portion of an object is \begin{equation}t_c=\frac{3\pi^2 n T}{\nu^3\omega_p^2}\sim 10^{-4} \,s, \quad n= \,number\,density,\end{equation} For a Janus ball, of radius 100 nm, half made of gold and half dielectric, with an initial temperature twice that of the background, the terminal velocity is only about 0.1 nm/s.
elements.sent-2411-061: For a spherical shell model with radius 1 cm and skin-depth thickness, $v_T$ would be about the same.
elements.section-2411-004: Spontaneous Torque on an Inhomogeneous Chiral Body
elements.sent-2411-063: In Sec.~\ref{sec2 , we calculated the torque in first order, which required the body be composed of nonreciprocal material, which usually necessitates an external field be applied.}
elements.sent-2411-064: In second order, a torque can arise on a reciprocal body, but again only if the body is {\it inhomogeneous .}
elements.sent-2411-065: It must further be {\it chiral , in that its mirror reflection cannot be turned into the original object by translations or rotations.}
elements.sent-2411-066: For a body with isotropic but inhomogeneous susceptibility there is only an external torque, calculated in second order: \begin{eqnarray}\tau_i = \int\frac{d\omega}{2\pi}\left(\coth\frac{\beta\omega}2 -\coth\frac{\beta'\omega}2\right)\epsilon_{ijk} \int(d\mathbf{r})(d\mathbf{r'})X(\mathbf{r,r'};\omega)\nonumber \qquad\times\Im\Gamma_{lm}(\mathbf{r-r'};\omega)r_j\nabla_k \Im\Gamma_{ml}(\mathbf{r'-r};\omega).\label{torque1}\end{eqnarray} Again the susceptibility product $X$ [Eq.~(\ref{X )] vanishes if the body is homogeneous.}
elements.sent-2411-067: If the body consists of two homogeneous parts, $A$ and $B$, $X$ is replaced by $X_{AB}$ defined in Eq.~(\ref{XAB ).}
elements.sent-2411-068: Using Eq.~(\ref{gradgg ), we find the torque in this situation is \begin{subequations \begin{equation}\bm\tau=\frac1{2\pi^2}\int_0^\infty \frac{d\omega}{2\pi}X_{AB}(\omega) \left(\frac1{e^{\beta\omega}-1}-\frac1{e^{\beta'\omega}-1}\right) \mathbf{J}_{AB}(\omega),\end{equation} in terms of the geometric factor \begin{equation}\mathbf{J}_{AB}(\omega)=-\int_A(d\mathbf{r})\int_B(d\mathbf{r'}) \frac{\mathbf{r\times r'}}{|\mathbf{r-r'}|^8}\phi(\omega|(\mathbf{r-r'}|). \label{gf}\end{equation} \end{subequations That the integral is convergent is evident from the behavior of $\phi$ given in Eq.~(\ref{limits ).}}}}
elements.sent-2411-069: A simple example of an inhomogeneous chiral object is shown in Fig.~\ref{figdaw , which illustrates what we might refer to as a dual Allen wrench.}
elements.figure-2411-005: \begin{minipage}{.45\textwidth} \includegraphics[width=\textwidth, trim = 1cm 19cm 11cm 1cm, clip]{chiral.pdf} \caption{ An inhomogeneous wire of small cross section bent in the shape of a dual Allen wrench. The end pieces (``tags'') $B$ are taken to be dispersionless dielectric, while the central piece $A$ is a Drude-type metal. The Cartesian coordinates of the various junctions are shown, as is the center of mass.} \label{figdaw} \end{minipage} \begin{minipage}{.5\textwidth} \includegraphics[width=\textwidth, trim = 1cm 19cm 11cm 1cm, clip]{dflag} \caption{Planar inhomogeneous object, obtained by replacing the tags by flags.} \vskip1.8cm \label{figdf} \end{minipage}
elements.sent-2411-070: This object will experience a quantum vacuum torque, but not a net force, because it is reflection invariant in the origin, $\mathbf{r\to-r}$.
elements.sent-2411-071: The geometric factor for this object, which points in the direction perpendicular to the plane of the object, is \begin{equation}J_{AB}(\omega)=2 S_AS_B\omega^8 \int_{-a}^a dy \int_0^b dx \, x y \frac{\phi(v)}{v^8}, \quad v=\omega\sqrt{x^2+(a+y)^2}.\end{equation}.
elements.formula-2411-037: \begin{equation}J_{AB}(\omega)=2 S_AS_B\omega^8 \int_{-a}^a dy \int_0^b dx \, x y \frac{\phi(v)}{v^8}, \quad v=\omega\sqrt{x^2+(a+y)^2}.\end{equation}
elements.figure-2411-006: \begin{minipage}{.45\textwidth} \includegraphics[width=5cm]{jab.pdf} \caption{Scaled geometric factor as a function of the length of the central wire, $\tilde{a}=a\omega$, for different values of $b=a/2$, $a$, or $2a$ from bottom to top. These are compared with the asymptotes for large- (dotted) and small- (dashed) arguments.} \label{figjab} \end{minipage} \begin{minipage}{.45\textwidth} \includegraphics[width=5cm]{HiT.pdf} \caption{Torque, apart from the prefactor, on a large Allen wrench, as a function of its temperature relative to the room-temperature background.} \vskip1cm \label{figlaw} \end{minipage}
elements.sent-2411-072: The large $\tilde{a}$ behavior is easily understood: the interactions between the parts are local, so increasing $b$ beyond a certain point does not increase the torque.

elements.sent-2411-074: The asymptotic value of $J_{AB}$, apart from the same prefactor, is $\hat{J _{AB}\sim 11\pi\omega a/30$ for $\omega a\gg1$.}

elements.sent-2411-081: Now, the corresponding torque is \begin{equation}\tau=\frac{28}{675\pi^3}\chi_B \nu^9\omega_p^2S_AS_B a^4b^2\hat{\tau}.\end{equation}.
elements.sent-2411-082: In this case, the terminal angular velocity is much larger, because the moment of inertia of the object is much smaller as well, \begin{equation}I=\rho_A S_A\frac23a^3+\rho_BS_B2b\left(a^2+\frac13 b^2\right).\end{equation}.
elements.sent-2411-083: Because the dimensionless torque is large, so is $\hat{\omega _T\sim 20,000$, if the initial temperature of the object is twice that of the room temperature environment, leading to a large terninal angular velocity, $\omega_T\sim 4\times 10^{-3}\, \mbox{s ^{-1}$, which should be quite observable.}}
elements.sent-2411-084: Further enhancement of the terminal angular velocity, by about a factor of $10^5$ for a large (1 cm) object or by about a factor of 10 for a small (1 $\mu$m) object, can be achieved by increasing the size of the tags, so we have a dual flag shown in Fig.~\ref{figdf .}
elements.sent-2411-085: Terminal angular velocities for both large and small dual flags would seem to be easily accessible to experimental observation.
elements.sent-2411-086: This is in contrast to the findings of Ref.~\citen{Reid .}
elements.sent-2411-087: The terminal angular velocity for a dual flag appears measurable.
